new_risk_factors: New U.S. tariffs on imports from China, India, Japan, South Korea, Taiwan, Vietnam, and the EU
Reciprocal and retaliatory tariffs from foreign governments
Section 232 investigation into semiconductor and derivative product imports
Supply chain disruption from tariffs including rare earths and raw materials availability
Pricing and gross margin impact from tariffs
Negative consumer sentiment from trade disputes
Currency headwinds (USD strength) impacting Americas net sales
Greater China 9-month iPhone net sales decline
positive_catalysts_cited: iOS 26, macOS Tahoe 26, iPadOS 26, watchOS 26, visionOS 26, and tvOS 26 announced in Q3 2025
Americas net sales growth driven by iPhone and Services
Europe net sales growth driven by Services and iPhone
Greater China Q3 net sales growth from iPhone and Mac
Japan net sales growth from iPhone and Services, aided by yen strength
Rest of Asia Pacific growth from Services and iPhone
key_quotes: Tariffs and other measures that are applied to the Company's products or their components can have a material adverse impact on the Company's business, results of operations and financial condition, including impacting the Company's supply chain, the availability of rare earths and other raw materials and components, pricing and gross margin
The ultimate impact remains uncertain and will depend on several factors, including whether additional or incremental U.S. Tariffs or other measures are announced or imposed

Department of Commerce has initiated an investigation under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962, as amended, into, among other things, imports of semiconductors, semiconductor manufacturing equipment, and their derivative products, including downstream products that contain semiconductors
Greater China net sales decreased during the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same period in 2024 due to lower net sales of iPhone, partially offset by higher net sales of Mac
payload.mda_summary: The most notable delta between Q2 and Q3 2025 MD&A language is the wholesale introduction of a dedicated 'Tariffs and Other Measures' risk section that was absent last quarter, escalating both the specificity and severity of trade-related cost and margin headwinds — particularly the Section 232 semiconductor investigation which threatens Apple's component supply chain at a structural level. Simultaneously, the removal of prior-quarter reassuring boilerplate (forward-looking statement safe harbors, seasonality framing, and macroeconomic context language) leaves the tariff risk block as one of the most prominent new disclosures, signaling management is treating this as a material and escalating concern rather than a generic macro footnote. Analysts may be underweighting the combination of Greater China iPhone weakness over the full nine-month period and the gross margin uncertainty introduced by tariff pass-through risk, especially given Apple's heavy reliance on Chinese manufacturing.
